Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it never really worked out that way. I'd end up scrolling through stuff online, getting distracted, and then cramming everything into late-night sessions when I couldn't avoid it anymore. With seven online courses, I was always juggling too much and nothing got the attention it deserved.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines rather than any real schedule. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then suddenly I'd have three things due and I'd be up until 3 AM trying to catch up. I kept meaning to set a regular routine, but between work and everything else going on, it just never stuck. The anxiety of it all made it worse because I'd avoid thinking about it until I absolutely had to.
Q3: Usually it was panic that got me to log in—like realizing something was due soon or getting an email reminder. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, but I wouldn't actually do much while I was there. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped a bit because there was something almost addictive about checking those progress bars, even if I wasn't completing much real work.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified elements were new and kind of interesting. But it never became a true habit for me. Life would get in the way—money stress, work shifts, just general exhaustion—and the routine would fall apart again. I'd go days without logging in and then feel too overwhelmed to catch up.
Q5: The reading materials were probably most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace, highlighting and making notes. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too when other students would explain things in simpler terms than the lectures. The gamified task system was weirdly motivating even though I knew it was kind of manipulating me into engaging more.
Q6: I barely watched any of the videos—like maybe 23 minutes total the whole course. They just felt too slow and I couldn't focus on them, especially with my anxiety making it hard to sit still. I'd skip ahead or just read the transcripts instead if they were available. A lot of the optional readings and supplementary stuff got ignored because I was barely keeping up with the required material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course or if it actually helped something click in my brain. The gamified tasks worked because they gave me that instant feedback, those little rewards that made me feel like I was accomplishing something even when I wasn't doing the big assignments. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my world. When you're stressed and barely keeping your head above water, optional means 'ignore this.' Required activities had deadlines attached, consequences, so they got attention—even if that attention came at the last possible minute. Optional activities felt like luxuries for students who had their lives together, which definitely wasn't me.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download readings and PDFs to my phone and go through them during my commute or breaks at work. I couldn't always afford good internet at home, so offline studying was kind of necessary. I'd also use free resources I found online—YouTube videos, Reddit explanations, random websites—because sometimes they explained things better than the course materials.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was studying from printed notes or downloaded materials while the LMS showed nothing. I'd be at work reading through stuff on my phone during slow periods, or I'd be going over my handwritten notes at home. None of that showed up anywhere. I also did a lot of just thinking through problems in my head, trying to understand concepts while doing other things.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd download something once, maybe skim it online, then do all my real studying with the offline version. My internet isn't always reliable and I couldn't always be at home to study, so having things saved locally was essential. The LMS probably thought I barely looked at stuff when really I'd gone through it multiple times offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was working extra shifts because money was tight, and I just felt completely overwhelmed by having seven courses going at once. There were weeks where I could barely make myself look at anything school-related because it just triggered this spiral of stress. The workload felt impossible and I started questioning why I was even doing this.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about bills, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Confusion was a big one too—if I didn't understand something, I'd avoid it rather than face feeling stupid. The fatigue was real; sometimes I'd log in, stare at the screen for a while, and then just close it because I couldn't process anything. My brain just wasn't there.
Q14: Oh, all the time. I'd have the LMS open, maybe click through some pages, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about money, thinking about work, just zoning out. I'd 'read' the same paragraph five times and retain nothing. Sometimes I was just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, even though I knew I wasn't actually learning anything.
Q15: The assignments were the big thing I avoided. My completion rate was terrible, like under 2%, which I'm not proud of. I'd look at an assignment, feel overwhelmed by it, tell myself I'd do it later, and then later never came until it was too late. The anxiety made it worse—the more I avoided something, the scarier it became, so I'd avoid it even more. It was a vicious cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I wouldn't do anything. With a deadline, I'd panic-work at the last minute. The problem was that my panic-work was often too little too late, especially for assignments. I'd focus on whatever was due soonest and let everything else slide. It wasn't a good system but it was the only way I functioned.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I didn't always engage with it the way I should have. Positive feedback gave me a brief boost, made me feel like maybe I could do this. Negative feedback or poor grades would send me into an avoidance spiral where I didn't want to face the course at all. The gamified feedback was easier to handle—less personal, more like a game score.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having things laid out clearly helped me know what I needed to do, but the sheer volume across seven courses was too much. I appreciated when modules were broken into smaller chunks because that felt more manageable. But overall, I think the structure assumed students had more time and fewer life complications than I did.
Q19: The gamified elements actually made engagement easier—they gave me small wins when everything else felt like failure. But the heavy reliance on videos made things harder for me since that's not how I learn. Also, having so many different platforms and tools to navigate was confusing and added to my stress. Simpler would have been better for someone like me who was already overwhelmed.
Q20: That's kind of me, actually. I logged in a lot, clicked around, engaged with the gamified stuff, participated in discussions here and there, but my actual assignment completion was abysmal. I think it's about the difference between low-stakes engagement and high-stakes work. Browsing and clicking feels safe; submitting work that gets judged feels terrifying, especially when you're already anxious and doubting yourself.
Q21: I can see that. I managed to pass despite my low engagement stats because I'm decent at exams and I did a lot of studying that didn't show up in the system. If you can read and retain information well, you don't necessarily need to do all the interactive stuff. Some people just learn differently, and the LMS doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials or external resources.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if your mind was elsewhere. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the discussions with friends about course material. They also can't capture the emotional side—like how anxiety might make someone avoid the platform even when they care about learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it, not just going through the motions. For me, real engagement happened in bursts—moments when something clicked or when I got genuinely interested in a topic. It's not about how many times you log in; it's about whether your brain is actually switched on when you're studying.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually cared about learning despite my terrible stats. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before deadlines or the hours I spent studying offline because I couldn't afford reliable internet. It wouldn't show that I was working part-time while taking seven courses, or that some days just surviving felt like an achievement. The numbers tell one story, but they're not the whole story.
